skill.name: code-review
skill.description: Guides AI assistants through structured code reviews focused on security, correctness, performance, readability, architecture, and test coverage.
skill.summary: Run structured code reviews with security, quality, performance, and architecture checklists.
skill.author: EricDuMingTong

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, crypto, network, and filesystem indicators, but review found they are mostly Markdown checklist entries, fenced code samples, or eval prompts. No prompt injection or malicious exfiltration intent was found. The remaining confirmed risk is intentional filesystem output because the skill tells the assistant to save a Markdown review file.

medium_findings.title: Intentional Review File Creation
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs the assistant to always save the completed review to a Markdown file. This is legitimate for a review workflow, but it creates workspace files and may derive filenames from commit, branch, PR, or diff context, so publication should disclose filesystem write behavior.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The instruction to save a Markdown file is explicit and confirmed in the skill text. Risk is moderate because no executable script is provided, but an AI agent following the skill may write files in the user workspace.

low_findings.title: Static Command Execution Findings Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: The reported Ruby backtick execution signals are Markdown inline code, fenced examples, eval prompts, or review checklist text. I did not find evidence of executable Ruby, shell scripts, or instructions to run these snippets as commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are prompts or Markdown examples, not executable files. The skill has no runnable code path that invokes shell commands.

low_findings.title: Security Keyword Findings Are Checklist Content
low_findings.description: Weak crypto, path traversal, reconnaissance, and credential-related hits appear in security review checklists as issues the assistant should detect in user code. They are not implemented by the skill itself.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious terms are part of educational audit criteria and removal-plan templates. No evidence found that the skill performs traversal, cryptography, reconnaissance, or credential access.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL Is Android XML Sample Text
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL findings are contained in UI component documentation samples, including Android XML namespace declarations and an avatar image example. No network request behavior is defined by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are sample XML attribute values and namespace declarations. There is no code that fetches, posts, or exfiltrates data to those URLs.

dangerous_patterns.title: Filesystem Write Instruction
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill asks the assistant to save the full review as a Markdown file after each review. Users should understand that enabling the skill may create review output files in the active workspace.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The behavior is directly stated in the output file section. It is not malicious, but it is a concrete side effect that should be visible to marketplace users.

content.user_title: Review Code for Security and Quality
content.value_statement: Manual code review can miss important security, correctness, and maintainability issues. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through a structured review with focused checklists and clear remediation advice.
actual_capabilities: Reviews code for security issues such as injection, XSS, SSRF, auth gaps, secret leakage, and weak crypto.
Checks correctness risks including null handling, boundary conditions, race conditions, and error handling.
Evaluates performance concerns such as N+1 queries, blocking I/O, unbounded collections, and missing caching.
Assesses readability, maintainability, SOLID design issues, and refactor opportunities.
Provides Android Nothing UI component consistency guidance when Android view or layout files are reviewed.
Saves the completed review to a Markdown file using a commit, patch, or default filename.
limitations: It depends on the code and context provided by the user.
It does not automatically run tests, scanners, or builds unless the host agent chooses to do so.
It may create a Markdown review file in the current workspace.
It is not a replacement for dedicated security testing or human approval on high-risk changes.

use_cases.title: Pre-merge Pull Request Review
use_cases.description: Find security, correctness, and maintainability risks before a branch is merged.

use_cases.title: Security-Focused Change Review
use_cases.description: Check authentication, authorization, input handling, secrets, and crypto choices in sensitive code.

use_cases.title: Architecture and Refactor Planning
use_cases.description: Identify overloaded modules, SOLID violations, dead code, and safe removal plans.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Code Check
prompt_templates.scenario: Use when you have a small snippet or single file.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this code for bugs, security issues, and readability problems. Prioritize the most important findings and explain how to fix them.

prompt_templates.title: Pull Request Review
prompt_templates.scenario: Use when reviewing a diff before merge.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this PR diff. Focus on changed lines, security impact, correctness regressions, performance risks, and missing tests. Provide prioritized findings.

prompt_templates.title: Security Review
prompt_templates.scenario: Use for authentication, data handling, or externally exposed code.
prompt_templates.prompt: Perform a security-focused review of this code. Check injection, XSS, SSRF, auth gaps, secret leakage, unsafe deserialization, weak crypto, and race conditions.

prompt_templates.title: Architecture Review
prompt_templates.scenario: Use for larger modules or refactor decisions.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this module for architecture, SOLID design, removal candidates, test gaps, and incremental refactor options. Separate urgent fixes from longer-term improvements.

output_examples.input: A pull request adds a new login endpoint and session cookie handling.
output: Critical security findings are listed first with file and line references.
The review explains why the session handling is risky and recommends safer controls.
Follow-up actions are grouped by urgency, including tests to add before merge.

output_examples.input: A backend service loops through orders and queries related records one by one.
output: The review identifies the N+1 query pattern and its performance impact.
It recommends batching or preloading related data.
It notes boundary cases and missing test coverage for empty inputs.

output_examples.input: An Android layout introduces standard widgets in a Nothing UI app.
output: The review checks whether available Nothing UI components should be used.
It separates consistency concerns from functional bugs.
It suggests minimal changes that preserve the existing layout intent.

best_practices: Provide the full diff or relevant files with line numbers when possible.
State the production context, framework, threat model, and test expectations.
Review the generated Markdown file before sharing it outside the workspace.
anti_patterns: Do not use it as the only approval gate for sensitive production changes.
Do not ask for exhaustive style feedback when urgent security bugs are present.
Do not rely on it without providing enough surrounding code and requirements.

faq.question: What does this skill review?
faq.answer: It reviews security, correctness, performance, readability, architecture, test coverage, and selected Android UI consistency concerns.

faq.question: Does it run code or execute tests?
faq.answer: No. The skill provides review instructions and checklists. Test execution depends on the host agent and user permission.

faq.question: Will it create files?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill instructs the assistant to save the full review as a Markdown file after completion.

faq.question: Can it review pull request diffs?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes guidance for reviewing changed lines while considering effects on surrounding code.

faq.question: Is it suitable for security reviews?
faq.answer: Yes, for assisted review. It should be paired with tests, scanners, and human review for high-risk systems.

faq.question: Which tools can use it?
faq.answer: The report marks it as compatible with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
